danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite-based hostile NPCs with simplified death animation
Stationary defensive structures in final area
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard blocking path to final boss; must be destroyed before progression

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger vehicle sprites in arcade version; set of 4 sequential turret placements in NES version
danger_signals.meaning: Major obstacle requiring sustained offensive action

danger_signals.visual_form: Final boss sprite with combat animations; originally depicted fleeing after turrets destroyed

danger_signals.meaning: Collateral damage penalty—danger to non-combatants indicated by large score reduction mechanic

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible sprites dropped by defeated soldiers
safety_signals.meaning: Power-up availability increases player offensive capability and survivability
Temporary armored protection and increased firepower

safety_signals.visual_form: Rail-based transition sequence with unique graphics
safety_signals.meaning: Safe passage/respite zone with reduced or no combat pressure

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Japanese version explicitly names them Guevara and Castro; international versions anonymize as Player 1/Player 2 to obscure Cuban Revolutionary connection
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Players are mobile, active initiators moving through enemy-controlled Caribbean island terrain

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Generic enemy soldiers with standardized sprite; Batista dictator as named final boss in Japanese version only; 'Dictator' in international releases
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Occupy fortified positions, rooftop turrets, vehicle patrols; control island infrastructure

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Visually distinct from soldiers (sprite differentiation); rescued/eliminated status affects score
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout combat zones; presence indicates contested territory and moral dimension of conflict

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy soldiers occupy all terrain; civilians present but threatened; fortified structures visible; title screen background (teal in Guevara, blue in Guerrilla War) establishes location
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continuous enemy patrol and combat engagement; hostages require rescue interaction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stages advance through different island locations (map screen with horizontal movement graphics planned but unused upward variant suggests multi-directional traversal design)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased enemy density and weapon availability as player advances; boss encounters mark area completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dictator/Batista sprite with fleeing animation (unused) indicating original defeat sequence; rooftop turret emplacements; final boss arena design
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turrets must be cleared before dictator engages; dictator originally fled after turrets destroyed, later revised to fight to death

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Credits roll with regional variations (North American version changed 'Fly Garuda' to 'Ninja Sasuke' and added 'Uncle Boo Boo'); Japanese version includes epilogue sequence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game state transitions to non-playable narrative conclusion

environmental_storytelling.example: 10 distinct areas/stages represent progression through island geography; minecart section in Area 5 (with unused larger sprite variants for player boarding) suggests traversal of varied terrain types
Standard soldiers vs. tank enemies vs. turret placements indicate escalating defensive infrastructure; larger tanks planned for arcade version replaced by sequential turret sets in NES port
Rooftop turrets on final area building block vertical progression; minecart passage requires different interaction mode (bonus level) rather than combat
Soldier drop-pickups (K, N, S, T powerups, with SNK/KNT easter egg arrangement in Stage 3) indicate equipment scavenging from defeated enemy forces
Hostages scattered throughout indicate oppressed population; rescue vs. casualty scoring system implies occupying force holds them as human shields or bargaining leverage

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Engage player on sight; yield weapons when defeated
Killed instantly by 'K' powerup, indicating group elimination mechanic
Fleeing sprite animations indicate escape sequence rather than combat
Fights to death after turret phase completion
Can be killed in crossfire (large score penalty) or rescued (score reward); indicates vulnerability to player negligence
Horizontal traversal with separate sprite (vertical variant unused); can enter/fall into minecart with unique animation frames

notes: Guerrilla War uses regional sanitization to obscure political content: Japanese 'Guevara' / 'Castro' character names became 'Player 1/Player 2' in Western releases; Dictator instead of Batista; unnamed island instead of Cuba. Visual communication relies on standard action-game hazard/safety iconography (enemies=danger, weapons=safety) rather than nuanced political symbology. The game's world-state progression is primarily narrative-driven (area-to-area advancement) with limited environmental visual shifts between states. Unused graphics (fleeing dictator, upward movement sprite, larger tanks, boss tank variant, expanded death animation, Sasuke vs. Commander minigame) indicate original vision included more environmental and behavioral variety than final NES version. Sprite reuse and palette limitations forced compromises (Castro lacks unique minecart tiles like Guevara; multi-directional movement visualized but not implemented). Enemy soldiers serve as generic threat indicators without faction-specific visual differentiation beyond placement context.
